summary: DeVault provides a practical introduction to Facebook's Flux architecture pattern paired with React.js, arguing that the upstream documentation is unnecessarily complex and brain-melting. He breaks down the three core components—dispatcher, stores, and views—with concrete code examples, emphasizing that Flux is far simpler than its documentation suggests. He addresses practical problems like async data loading and the lack of batteries-included tooling. The post concludes with his conviction that React and Flux represent the right way to build frontends, replacing the MVC model that was shoehorned into client-side development.
key_points: Flux's unidirectional data flow (dispatcher → stores → views) replaces the MVC pattern that was awkwardly adapted from server-side development
The architecture is fundamentally simple despite intimidating documentation: stores own data, views render it, and the dispatcher routes actions between them
Views should never directly mutate data—all changes flow through the dispatcher to stores, which raise events that trigger re-renders
Flux provides almost no usable code out of the box—just a dispatcher—requiring developers to roll their own event handling and base store classes
money_quotes: The MVC model was strong on the server when we decided to take the frontend seriously, and it was shoehorned into the frontend since we didn't know any better.
At the core of it, React and Flux are very simple and elegant. Far more simple than the voodoo sales pitch upstream would have you believe.
There is one dispatcher and one set of callbacks, and everything that registers for it will receive every action given to the dispatcher, and can do with this as it pleases.
Yeah, that's all there is to it.
Stores end up having a lot of boilerplate. I haven't quite figured out the best way to address that yet.
React and Flux are going to be big. This feels like the right way to build a frontend.
key_insight: Flux's unidirectional data flow is a simple and elegant replacement for MVC on the frontend, obscured mainly by its own overwrought documentation.
